summary:
The Tang-Tibet relations (c. 618–842 AD) constituted one of the defining geopolitical rivalries of medieval Central Asia, as the [[event:87|Tang Dynasty China]] and the newly unified [[event:2190|Tibetan Empire]] competed for control of the Silk Road oasis cities and the strategic mountain passes connecting Central Asia with South and East Asia. The relationship began diplomatically: the Tibetan emperor Songtsen Gampo married a Tang princess (Princess Wencheng, 641 AD) as well as a Nepali princess, and the Buddhist cultural influences these marriages brought are credited in Tibetan tradition with laying the foundations for Buddhism's introduction to Tibet.

The Tang-Tibetan relationship quickly became militarily contentious as both empires expanded into Central Asia. Tibet at its height (c. 700–800 AD) controlled territory stretching from Yunnan in the east to Central Asian oasis cities like Kashgar in the northwest, and briefly occupied the Tang capital Chang'an itself in 763 AD during a period of Tang weakness following the [[event:2153|An Lushan Rebellion]]. The two powers fought recurrently over the Tarim Basin oasis cities, with control of Dunhuang — the critical Silk Road hub — changing hands multiple times. The rivalry was eventually resolved not by military victory but by the collapse of both empires: Tang declined after An Lushan, while the Tibetan Empire fragmented after the assassination of the anti-Buddhist king Langdarma in 842 AD. The cultural legacy of this era was enormous: Buddhist art and manuscripts preserved at Dunhuang — the Dunhuang caves — document the cultural mixing of Tang, Tibetan, and Central Asian traditions during this period.
